title: The Forest Before Timber

features.name: spiral-bark
features.desc-on-touch: Trees carved with patterns that spiral without returning to origin, the stone-carver's knife performing what forests do naturally. You're reading the forest's conversation with itself.

features.name: before-the-saw
features.desc-on-touch: The moment when the forest agreed it would become timber. The archive holds the forest's side of the conversation—bark-language nobody has yet learned to read.

features.name: unmarked-survey
features.desc-on-touch: Notes from a surveyor who refused to measure. The blank pages are as detailed as any description: what the forest meant by silence.

features.name: timber-rights-archive
features.desc-on-touch: Legal documents that named the forest into property. The archive holds them alongside the forest's own records: one list divides, one remembers wholeness.

features.name: root-still-remembering
features.desc-on-touch: Deep in the soil, the network of roots continues the forest's thought. They move without boundaries, comparing what the trees above have forgotten.

inscription: here is where the forest was still alive and the boundary was still becoming fence

file.name: Pre_Division_Forest_Spiral_Notation_Undated.txt
file.note: Carved markings from eucalyptus stand. Notation system appears to chart growth patterns or territorial claims. Archive uncertain: might predate all written language in the region.

exits.label: downstream to the mill where the division was agreed
deeper upstream to where the forest has no name yet
toward the older trees that remember being unlogged
